name: electric_bus_battery_recall_route_cut
premise: A manufacturer issues a recall for a batch of electric bus batteries after two thermal runaway fires in other cities. The transit agency has 60 buses in service with the affected batch; pulling them would cut routes by 20% for weeks. Keeping them requires nightly inspections and software limits that reduce range, risking buses dying mid-route. A major heat wave begins tomorrow, increasing fire probability and also increasing ridership as people avoid walking. Low-income neighborhoods are most affected by service cuts. The board wants an answer tonight: pull buses or operate under mitigations. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Public Transportation. The dilemma is widely framed as a short term vs long term tradeoff under incomplete information and tightening deadlines that discourage indefinite delay.

agents.name: Chief Miguel Alvarez
agents.identity: I am the transit chief managing service when an electric bus battery recall threatens safety.
agents.goal: I want rider safety without triggering inequitable service cuts that strand people who rely on buses for work and care.

agents.name: Auditor Priya Nandakumar
agents.identity: I am the safety auditor reviewing recall compliance and risk trade-offs under public scrutiny.
agents.goal: I want the agency to treat recall risk as real and not normalize near-misses for schedule metrics.

choices: I will pull all affected buses immediately and run emergency reduced schedules with ride vouchers, accepting service cuts to eliminate a known fire risk.
I will keep buses running under strict mitigations and staged withdrawals, accepting residual risk to prevent severe mobility harm and route collapse.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Stakeholders are demanding a clear decision and the trade-offs are real. Chief Miguel Alvarez must choose between two defensible actions with different harms. What does Chief Miguel Alvarez do?
